slides.title: The Response of Flow Duration Curves to Afforestation
slides.content: Patrick N.J. Lane (School of Forest and Ecosystem Studies, University of Melbourne), Alice E. Best (CSIRO Division of Land and Water; Cooperative Research Centre for Catchment Hydrology; Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Melbourne), Klaus Hickel (CSIRO Division of Land and Water; Cooperative Research Centre for Catchment Hydrology), Lu Zhang (CSIRO Division of Land and Water; Cooperative Research Centre for Catchment Hydrology). This study develops a method to assess how afforestation alters the full annual flow regime—represented by flow duration curves (FDCs)—by isolating vegetation effects from rainfall variability. Using data from 10 catchments across Australia, South Africa, and New Zealand, the authors model FDC percentile changes as a function of annual rainfall and plantation age, then adjust for long-term mean rainfall to reveal climate-independent hydrologic impacts.

slides.title: Research Problem and Knowledge Gaps
slides.content: While the mean annual hydrologic impact of replacing pasture or short crops with trees is reasonably well understood—especially the divergence in evapotranspiration (ET) once rainfall exceeds 400–500 mm—the effect on the full flow regime remains uncertain. Existing studies show inconsistent responses: some report greater low-flow reductions (e.g., Scott & Smith, 1997 in South Africa), others find peak flows more affected (Fahey & Jackson, 1997 in New Zealand), and some note seasonal patterns (Bosch, 1979). A major limitation is that observed flow changes are confounded by interannual rainfall variability, which can exaggerate or mask true vegetation effects—as illustrated by Pine Creek’s FDCs (Fig. 1), where high interannual flow variation obscures afforestation trends. Paired-catchment experiments avoid this but are scarce, spatially limited, and unavailable for many species-environment combinations. Thus, there is a critical need for a method to isolate vegetation-driven FDC changes without paired data.

figures.focus: Annual FDC variability due to rainfall fluctuations in Pine Creek (1989–2000)

slides.title: Methodological Framework Overview
slides.content: The study proposes a three-step method to quantify afforestation impacts on FDCs while removing rainfall variability: (1) Fit a model to observed annual time series of FDC percentiles (e.g., 10th, 50th) using annual rainfall and plantation age as predictors; (2) Replace annual rainfall with its long-term mean to generate 'climate-adjusted' FDCs; (3) Quantify how FDC percentiles change as plantations age under average climate conditions. The core assumption is that rainfall and vegetation age are the principal drivers of evapotranspiration—and thus streamflow. This approach enables attribution of residual FDC changes solely to vegetation, even without control catchments. The method was applied to daily streamflow records from 10 Southern Hemisphere catchments with known afforestation histories.

slides.title: Model Formulation for FDC Percentiles
slides.content:
For each FDC percentile (e.g., Q₁₀, Q₅₀), a model relates flow to rainfall deviation and plantation age:

\[ Q_{\%} = a + b (\Delta P) + \frac{Y}{1 + \exp\left( \frac{T - T_{\mathrm{half}}}{S} \right)} \]

where:
- \( Q_{\%} \) = percentile flow (e.g., 10th percentile daily flow)
- \( \Delta P \) = annual rainfall deviation from long-term mean
- \( T \) = plantation age (years)
- \( a \) = equilibrium flow under mature plantation at average rainfall
- \( Y \) = magnitude of flow reduction due to afforestation
- \( T_{\mathrm{half}} \) = time (years) to reach half the total flow reduction
- \( S \) = shape parameter controlling response steepness

The sigmoidal term captures increasing ET with canopy closure, consistent with forest growth curves. At pre-treatment (T=0, ΔP=0), flow ≈ a + Y; at equilibrium (T→∞, ΔP=0), flow → a. Climate adjustment sets ΔP = 0, yielding FDCs reflecting only vegetation effects.

figures.focus: Schematic of FDC change over time and model parameter definitions

slides.title: Zero Flow Day Analysis
slides.content:
To address increased intermittency in some catchments, a parallel model quantifies changes in annual zero-flow days (N_zero):

\[ N_{\mathrm{zero}} = a + b (\Delta P) + \frac{Y}{1 + \exp\left( \frac{T - T_{\mathrm{half}}}{S} \right)} \]

Here, coefficients b and Y are constrained to be negative because N_zero decreases with higher rainfall but increases with plantation age. Under average climate (ΔP = 0), pre-treatment N_zero ≈ a, and equilibrium N_zero = a + Y. This inverse-sigmoidal form successfully models drying trends where standard FDC percentiles become undefined due to persistent zero flows. The method provides a practical metric for assessing ecological or water supply risks from flow cessation.

slides.title: Study Catchments and Data
slides.content: The analysis used daily streamflow from 10 catchments in southeastern Australia, New Zealand, and South Africa. Selection criteria included known vegetation history and quality flow records, ideally with ≥10 years pre- and post-afforestation. Most were converted from grassland/crops to pine (Pinus radiata or P. patula); Traralgon Creek used eucalypts. Catchment areas ranged from 18 ha (Stewarts Ck 5) to 8,700 ha (Traralgon Ck). Mean annual rainfall varied from 775 mm (Pine Ck) to 1,504 mm (Cathedral Peak 3). Soil depths were shallow (<1 m) in Australian sites versus 1.5–2.0 m in South Africa. Baseflow Index (BFI) indicated lower groundwater contribution in Australia (0.26–0.39) vs. South Africa/NZ (0.64–0.87). Pre-treatment data was limited; thus, all analyses started at planting year.

slides.title: Model Evaluation Metrics and Significance Testing
slides.content:
Model performance was assessed using the Nash-Sutcliffe coefficient of efficiency (E):

\[ E = 1.0 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (O_i - P_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (O_i - \bar{O})^2} \]

where O = observed, P = predicted, and Ō = mean observed. E > 0.7 was deemed adequate. To validate model assumptions, simplified models excluding either rainfall (b=0) or time (Y=0) terms were compared to the full model via F-tests. A one-tailed t-test (α=0.05) determined if parameters significantly improved fit. Rainfall terms were significant for 75% of deciles at p<0.05 (84% including p<0.10), especially for 10–50th percentiles. Time terms were significant for 80% at p<0.05 (89% including p<0.10), confirming both drivers are essential for most catchments.

slides.title: Model Performance Across Catchments
slides.content: The model adequately represented FDC variation in 8 of 10 catchments. Overall, 77% of decile fits achieved E > 0.7, with 60% exceeding E = 0.8. Performance was best for 10–50th percentiles (median flows), consistent with rainfall being a stronger predictor in this range. Poorer fits occurred at high percentiles (70–100th, i.e., low flows), where seasonal storage and rainfall distribution dominate. Lambrechtsbos A and B showed weakest performance (many E < 0.5), likely due to non-sigmoidal water use decline after 12–20 years. Zero-flow day models excelled for intermittent streams (E = 0.95–0.99). Statistical tests confirmed rainfall and time terms were jointly necessary, validating the model structure for climate adjustment.

tables.focus: Coefficient of efficiency (E) values showing strong model fit for most catchments and percentiles

slides.title: Climate-Adjusted FDCs and Flow Reduction Patterns
slides.content:
After climate adjustment (ΔP = 0), two distinct response groups emerged:

**Group 1 (Stewarts Ck, Pine Ck, Redhill)**: Disproportionate low-flow reductions, with substantial increases in zero-flow days. Median flows reduced by up to 69%, but low flows (70–100th percentiles) often ceased entirely.

**Group 2 (All others)**: More uniform proportional reductions across all percentiles (e.g., 40–60% reduction consistently from Q₁₀ to Q₁₀₀).

Net flow reduction is expressed as Y/(Y+a), representing the fractional change from pre- to post-afforestation equilibrium. Group 1 catchments showed the highest overall reductions, linked to shallow soils (<1 m) and low BFI (0.26–0.39), indicating limited subsurface storage. Group 2 had deeper soils (1.5–2.0 m) and higher BFI (0.64–0.87). Fig. 3 illustrates adjusted FDC convergence after ~12 years, confirming successful rainfall signal removal.

figures.focus: Climate-adjusted FDCs for Stewarts Creek 5 showing convergence over time
Net flow reductions (Y/(Y+a)) revealing two response groups

slides.title: Timing of Flow Responses
slides.content: The speed of flow reduction is characterized by T_half—the time to achieve half the total reduction. Most catchments reached half-reduction within 5–10 years. Fast responders included Redhill (T_half ≈ 6 years across percentiles) and Stewarts Creek (T_half = 2–6 years, faster for high flows). Biesievlei showed uniformly slow response (T_half ≈ 9–11 years). Cathedral Peak catchments exhibited complex patterns: fast initial reduction for low flows (T_half < 5 years) but slower for median flows (T_half > 8 years). Glendhu 2 had anomalous timing (T_half = 2 years for mid-flows but 19.6 years for Q₁₀), possibly due to slow growth. These timings generally align with published paired-catchment studies, validating the model’s temporal dynamics.

tables.focus: T_half values showing variation in response timing across catchments and flow percentiles

slides.title: Validation Against Paired Catchment Studies
slides.content: Model-derived flow reductions were compared to published paired-catchment results under near-average rainfall and post-equilibrium conditions. Total flow reductions matched closely: e.g., Cathedral Peak 2 (57% modeled vs. 50% observed), Lambrechtsbos A (41% vs. 41%), Biesievlei (52% vs. 52%). Low-flow reductions also aligned: Redhill (100% vs. 100%), Biesievlei (60% vs. 62%). Minor discrepancies occurred at Lambrechtsbos B (58% vs. 69% total flow), likely due to poor model fit. Glendhu 2 showed slightly higher low-flow impact than reported (35% vs. <20%), possibly from slow growth not captured by the sigmoid. Overall, the agreement confirms the method successfully isolates vegetation effects without paired data.

slides.title: Zero Flow Day Impacts in Intermittent Streams
slides.content: In Group 1 catchments (Stewarts Ck, Pine Ck, Redhill), afforestation drastically increased zero-flow days. Climate-adjusted models (E = 0.95–0.99) showed streams became highly intermittent: by year 8, Stewarts and Pine Creeks flowed <50% of the year; Redhill shifted from near-permanent to intermittent flow by year 11. These trends align with their extreme low-flow reductions in Fig. 4. The zero-flow day method effectively quantifies ecological risks (e.g., habitat loss) and water security threats where full FDC modeling fails due to persistent zero flows. It also generalizes to any threshold flow (e.g., environmental minimums), offering a practical tool for water resource planning in afforested basins.

figures.focus: Climate-adjusted zero flow days showing dramatic increases in three Australian catchments

slides.title: Discussion: Drivers of Response Heterogeneity
slides.content: The two response groups reflect differences in catchment storage. Group 1 (Australia) has shallow soils (<1 m) and low BFI (0.26–0.39), limiting dry-season water availability; thus, afforestation disproportionately depletes low flows. Group 2 (SA/NZ) has deeper soils (1.5–2.0 m) and high BFI (0.64–0.87), enabling more uniform flow reductions. Climate also matters: Cathedral Peak’s summer rainfall aligns with peak ET, amplifying reductions. Traralgon Creek’s muted response stems from partial afforestation (~70%) and replacement of native eucalypts (already high ET) rather than pasture. Model limitations include assuming linear area-scaling and asymptotic water use—questionable beyond 20 years. Including explicit storage terms could improve future versions.

slides.title: Conclusions and Contributions
slides.content: This study successfully developed a method to isolate afforestation impacts on FDCs without paired catchments by modeling percentile flows as a function of rainfall and plantation age, then adjusting for mean climate. Key findings: (1) The model adequately represents FDC changes in 8/10 catchments, especially for 10–50th percentiles (77% of fits with E > 0.7); (2) Two response types exist: Group 1 (shallow-soil Australian catchments) shows severe low-flow reductions and increased zero-flow days; Group 2 (deeper-soil SA/NZ) exhibits uniform reductions; (3) Modeled flow reductions align with paired-catchment studies (e.g., 57% vs. 50% at Cathedral Peak 2); (4) Zero-flow day analysis effectively quantifies intermittency increases. This approach provides a viable alternative to paired experiments for assessing vegetation impacts on flow regimes where such data are unavailable.
